title: Gift-Based Ministry
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-26-24 -PM- Gift-Based Ministry - 1 Peter 4_10-11 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: We Are Made to Live in Community
subpoints: The 38-plus "one another" commands in the Bible testify that we were made and recreated to live in community
Review of this morning's passage: the four exhortations — prayer, fervent love, hospitality, ministering gifts
The basis: the end of all things is at hand; we invest in the people of the age to come
The goal: that God may be glorified through Jesus Christ in all things

outline.title: Every Christian Has Received a Gift
subpoints: Verse 10: "As each one has received a gift" — this is a general statement about all Christians
A spiritual gift is any talent or ability empowered by the Holy Spirit and able to be used in the ministry of the church
These are gracious gifts — charisma contains charis (grace); they are given, not earned
They are given to manage and return, not to keep — the parable of the talents
No room for pride — since the gift is grace, your ability is not to your credit
General gifts that all Christians possess: prayer, love, knowledge of the Bible, the ability to speak the Word in some capacity

outline.title: How to Discover Your Gifts
subpoints: What do you like doing, and can you do it for the glory of God?
What do the people around you say you are good at, and can that be used for God's glory and to bless others?
Romans 12:3-8: a non-exhaustive list of gifts — prophecy, ministry, teaching, exhortation, giving, leading, showing mercy
All Christians share in every gift to some degree; some have a greater aptitude in certain areas
1 Corinthians 12:4-11: God has great creativity in gifting His church; all gifts are for the profit of all
Do not despise any gift — the Corinthians wanted only visible, up-front gifts; Paul corrects this

outline.title: Using the Gift of Speaking in the Church
subpoints: "If anyone speaks, let him speak as the oracles of God" — speaking must be biblically informed and God-honoring
Applied to any teaching role: content must be drawn from and consistent with the Word of God
Your life must not contradict what the Bible teaches; you must submit to the teaching authority of the elders
Hebrews 13:17: one of the worst things you can do spiritually is make the elders' work more difficult by gossiping, undermining, or not listening
Not just formal teaching — all speech in the community of believers should be saturated with the Word (Colossians 3:16)

outline.title: Using the Gift of Serving in the Church
subpoints: "If anyone ministers, let him do it as with the ability which God supplies" — serve by God's grace, not willpower alone
Serving by your own strength leads to burnout and bitterness; serving by God's grace is sustained
No limit to what we can do to serve each other except the Word of God as guardrails
Examples: babysitting, cooking, cleaning, visiting, organizing, finances — all for the glory of God to bless people
The purpose of God's gift of service: the building up of the body and conformity to Christ

outline.title: The Goal: Growing Together into the Fullness of Christ (Ephesians 4:7-16)
subpoints: Ephesians 4:7-8 quotes Psalm 68: when Christ ascended, He gave gifts to men — the teaching offices of the church
Purpose: for the equipping of the saints, unto the work of the ministry
Goal: that we all come to the unity of the faith, to a perfect man, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ
Ephesians 4:15-16: the whole body is joined and knit together, every part exercising its gift, causing growth in love
A healthy church is one where every part exercises its gifts so that all grow together into the fullness of Christ

summary:
In this Sunday evening sermon on 1 Peter 4:10-11, Pastor Tito Lyro focuses on the concept of gift-based ministry — the principle that God has given every member of the church a grace-gift to be used in serving others. He builds on the morning sermon's treatment of 1 Peter 4:7-11, noting that all Christians are made for community, and the 38-plus "one another" commands in Scripture make this undeniable. The basis is eschatological: everything of this age will pass away, but the people of God are citizens of the age to come, so we invest in each other.

Every Christian has received a gift (charisma — a word that contains charis, "grace"), which means these gifts are not earned but given. They belong not to the recipient but to God, to be managed and returned like the talents in the parable. This eliminates all pride. How does one discover one's gifts? Ask what you love doing and can do for God's glory; ask what the people around you affirm you are good at. Romans 12:3-8 and 1 Corinthians 12:4-11 both give non-exhaustive lists: prophecy, ministry, teaching, exhortation, giving, leading, mercy, and more. Every Christian shares in all these gifts to some degree, with some having a greater aptitude in particular areas. God has great creative diversity in equipping His church.

Pastor Lyro then applies Peter's two examples. The gift of speaking must be exercised as the oracles of God — only what Scripture teaches, with a life consistent with those teachings, submitted to the authority of the elders. The gift of serving must be exercised by the ability God supplies, not by willpower alone — otherwise burnout and bitterness follow. The sermon closes with Ephesians 4:7-16: when Christ ascended He distributed gifts through the teaching offices of the church, so that the whole body would be joined and knit together, every part doing its share, causing the church to grow up in all things into Christ. A healthy church is one where every part is exercising its gifts for the fullness of their Head.
transcript:
## Introduction: We Are Made to Live in Community

To first Peter chapter 4, as we continue our series on first Peter. Some of what we're going to consider this afternoon will be a little bit of review from this morning, but I want to build on what we talked about this morning as far as being gifted by God towards ministry, and to consider the idea that we're supposed to have a gift-based ministry — that we should serve according to the gifts that God has given us, and that's how we should do things as a church: aligning people with service that they are gifted to do.

The fact that God has saved us to live in community with one another cannot be denied by an honest reader of the Scriptures. The 38-plus "one another" commands that we find in the Bible alone tells us that we were made and we were recreated to live in community with each other in the church of Jesus Christ. And this passage is one of these undeniable calls to live in community through Jesus Christ.

So we're going to read verses 7 through 11 again of chapter 4. This is the word of our Lord, 1 Peter 4:7: "But the end of all things is at hand; therefore be serious and watchful in your prayers. And above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins. Be hospitable to one another without grumbling. As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another, as good stewards of the manifold grace of God. If anyone speaks, let him speak as the oracles of God. If anyone ministers, let him do it as with the ability which God supplies, that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belong the glory and the dominion forever and ever. Amen."

Let us pray. Father, pray that You would enlighten our hearts that we might see glorious things concerning You in the passage. Enable me to be a blessing to Your people this afternoon. In Jesus's name. Amen.

We're going to concentrate on verses 10 and 11 this afternoon. Verse 11 is part of the fourth exhortation in this passage. In verse 7 we are told to be serious and watchful in our prayers. In verse 8 we have fervent love for one another. In verse 9 we have to be hospitable. And then in verse 10 we're called to minister to one another, and verse 11 gives us two examples of how that ministry looks.

As we saw this morning, the reason for this ministry is that the end of all things is at hand in verse 7. All the events of the history of redemption — creation, fall, Abraham, Exodus, the Old Testament prophets, the Old Testament kingdom, the coming of Christ, death, resurrection, ascension, session — have happened already. The only thing now missing is the coming again of the Lord Jesus Christ. That's where we are living — the period in which the only thing missing is the coming of the Lord Jesus Christ when the church is gathered.

So we live in the light of the truth that this current world order, this age, will soon pass away. Everything we see around us will be soon passed away. So we focus our ministry on things that are characteristic of the age to come, which is loving one another. And we especially focus on the people of the age to come — everything of this age will pass away; the only thing that won't is us, because we are citizens of the age to come. We are sojourners, pilgrims, foreigners to this age. We are citizens of the age to come. So we invest in those people from the age to come, which is each other. We minister to one another.

And as we saw this morning, Peter gives the goal of these things: the glory of God through Jesus Christ in verse 11. It is through Jesus Christ that we glorify God by serving in ministry to one another, and this type of work can only be done through faith in Jesus Christ and reliance on Him to sustain us in it.

## Every Christian Has Received a Gift

As I mentioned this morning, these gifts listed here are gracious gifts. The word for gift — charisma — includes the word for grace — charis. These are gifts given to us by the grace of God. They are given by the Savior to those who have been transformed by His grace. They are not merit-based but grace-based. We're not able to serve because we work really hard at it — though we're supposed to work hard at serving — we're able to serve because God has given us the grace to do so.

And that's why the first exhortation is to faithfulness and diligence in prayer, because without the strength of the Lord we're not going to be able to serve each other as He calls us to do.

As I said this morning, in verse 10 Peter tells us: "As each one has received a gift" — this is a general statement. Each one in the church of Jesus Christ has received a gift. God has given each one of you abilities, talents, gifts. These are not ours to keep, but God's for us to manage. God has given to you — all of you — something that you can use to bless somebody with, something that you can use to serve somebody. And that was not given to you for you to keep — it was given to you to manage it and return to the Lord at His coming, just like the parable of the talents.

And since our abilities are a gift, there is no room for pride in this. So what are these gifts?

A spiritual gift is any talent or ability that is empowered by the Holy Spirit and able to be used in the ministry of the church. So how do we find out what our gifts are?

There are general gifts that everyone has. Every Christian has the ability to pray. Every Christian has the ability to love. Every Christian has the ability to know the Bible. Every Christian has the ability to speak the Bible in some form. So I'm telling you — you all have at least these four gifts from the Lord, to be used to serve in the church.

Remember what I told you this morning about Colossians 3:16 where Paul commands us to let the Word of Christ dwell richly in us — and then describes how that's done: by speaking to one another in psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, singing with grace in your hearts to the Lord. The Word of God dwells richly in us when we pour it into others. All of us have this gift of speaking the Word of God, and we need to exercise it for the good of each other and the glory of God.

## How to Discover Your Gifts

Now how about some person-specific gifts? What gift has God given to you specifically? Well, ask yourself: what do I like doing, and can I do that for the glory of God? That's one way to figure out how God has equipped you. What do the people around you say you're good at, and can that be used for the glory of God? Can you use these things to bless others in the name of Jesus Christ? Most of the time the answer is going to be yes, and we just have to go and do what God calls us to do.

If you want some fuel for brainstorming concerning your gifts, turn to Romans 12 starting at verse 3. Paul says: "For I say, through the grace given to me, to everyone who is among you: do not think of yourself more highly than you ought to think, but to think soberly, as God has dealt to each one a measure of faith." So one gift that we all have but don't all exercise is the gift of humility. There is no spiritual gift of pride — the gift is humility and all of us have it, we just need to exercise it.

Continuing: "For as we have many members in one body, but all the members do not have the same function, so we, being many, are one body in Christ, and individually members of one another, having then gifts differing according to the grace that is given to us. Let us use them. If prophecy, let us prophesy in proportion to our faith; or ministry, let us use it in our ministry; he who teaches, in his teaching; he who exhorts, in his exhortation; he who gives, with liberality; he who leads, with diligence; he who shows mercy, with cheerfulness."

These are common gifts that all share, but some are better at it than others. We all have been given the gift of prophecy — and I'm not going charismatic. Prophecy in the New Testament includes the idea of being able to proclaim the Word of God. That's why the Puritans called one of their most famous books on preaching "The Art of Prophesying" by Perkins. So that's why we've all been given the gift of prophecy — to proclaim the Word of God. Some of us may have a greater aptitude for doing that.

We all have been given the gift of ministry — the ability to minister to one another. Some of us are more talented at it. All of us have been given the gift of teaching in some capacity, because all of us are called to explain the gospel. But some may have a greater ability to teach.

All of us have been called to exhort — to encourage one another and point each other to Christ. Some of us may be better at doing that than others. All of us have been called to give, though some give with more liberality. All of us can lead in some capacity, though some are able to do it more easily. All of us can show mercy, though some have an easier time showing it.

So here you have just to start your brains going — a non-exhaustive list. There is no exhaustive list of gifts in the Bible.

Turn to 1 Corinthians 12, starting at verse 4. "There are diversities of gifts, but the same Spirit. There are differences of ministries, but the same Lord. And there are diversities of activities, but it is the same God who works all in all. But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to each one for the profit of all."

So you see that whatever gift God has given to you is for the profit of all. "For to one is given the word of wisdom through the Spirit, to another the word of knowledge through the same Spirit, to another faith by the same Spirit, to another gifts of healings by the same Spirit, to another the working of miracles, to another prophecy, to another discerning of spirits, to another different kinds of tongues, to another the interpretation of tongues. But one and the same Spirit works all these things, distributing to each one individually as He wills."

Not all these gifts are for us today, but we can see the diversity of gifts and God's creative activity toward the church. God equips His church for every era of its existence, and He has great creativity in gifting His church. The church needs that diversity — all these parts need to be present.

And later in the same chapter, Paul encourages us not to disregard any gift. The Corinthians wanted the gifts that attracted the most attention to themselves — the gifts that put them up front, speaking to everybody where everybody could see. And Paul says: there are all kinds of other things that need to be done that are important as well. We have to be wanting all the gifts, not just some.

## Using the Gift of Speaking in the Church

Back in 1 Peter 4, verse 11, Peter highlights these two gifts — speaking and serving — because the minister is to serve. If you think you're called to serve by speaking to the body of Christ, make sure that what you're saying is true according to the Word of God and that you are doing it in obedience to the Word of God.

If you think you're being called to speak to the church, make sure that you're doing it because you want to honor God and bless people, not because you just want to be in front of people. That is to say: your encouragement of others, your teaching, your counseling, your singing — all should be biblically informed, with the desire to glorify God.

This means that the Bible content must be second nature. If you think you have the gift of speaking to the body of Christ, you need to know the Bible. You need to study. You need to be prepared. There is no shortcut to it.

It also means that you submit yourself to the teaching of God's Word. Your life will not contradict what the Bible teaches you. And you will teach in a manner consistent with what the Bible teaches — for example, in a way that does not undermine the authority of the elders. Hebrews 13:17 says: "Obey those who rule over you, and be submissive, for they watch out for your souls as those who must give account. Let them do so with joy and not with grief, for that would be unprofitable for you."

As a side note: one of the worst things you can do spiritually for yourself is to make the job of the elders more difficult. Complaining, gossiping, undermining, going against what they say, not listening to them — you are shooting yourself in the head spiritually by doing that. No benefit to anyone, least of all yourself.

## Using the Gift of Serving in the Church

Now, if you think that you're gifted to serve others by doing things for them, well — do that by the grace of God. Babysitting, cooking, cleaning, visiting, helping, organizing finances — the list could go on and on. But God calls you to do that by His grace.

If you try to do that on your own, you will burn out and may become bitter toward the people you're trying to serve. But by relying on the grace of God, you are empowered by the Spirit to love others, and it will be God working through the service — which is less likely to make you bitter.

And brothers and sisters, there is no limit to what we can do to serve each other other than the guardrails of the Word of God. Anything within those guardrails, we can use to serve each other.

And the purpose of God's gift of service in this life is the building up of the body and its conformity to Christ.

## The Goal: Growing Together into the Fullness of Christ

I'd like to turn to one more passage as we finish here this afternoon — Ephesians 4 starting at verse 7. The Apostle says: "To each one of us grace was given according to the measure of Christ's gift." Again, to each one of us, grace was given by Christ according to the measure of Christ's gift. Verse 8: "Therefore He says: 'When He ascended on high, He led captivity captive, and gave gifts to men.'" This quote is from Psalm 68, which was read this morning.

Why does it say He ascended? "He also first descended into the lower parts of the earth" — meaning, He came down to earth. "He who descended is also the One who ascended far above all the heavens, that He might fill all things." And He Himself gave some to be apostles, some prophets, some evangelists, and some pastors and teachers, for the equipping of the saints for the work of the ministry.

So Christ gifted His church with teachers — not just so the church can hear good teaching. Not just so that Pastor T can say interesting things. But so that the church can be equipped in order to do the work of the ministry. And as the church together exercises its gifts in the work of the ministry, it arrives at — verse 13 — the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God, to a perfect man, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ.

"That we should no longer be children tossed to and fro and carried about with every wind of doctrine, by the trickery of men, in the cunning craftiness of deceitful plotting, but, speaking the truth in love, may grow up in all things into Him who is the head — Christ."

How does that happen? It happens as we exercise the gifts that God has given us in loving and serving one another. Verse 16: "From whom the whole body, joined and knit together by what every joint supplies, according to the effective working by which every part does its share, causes growth of the body for the edifying of itself in love."

A healthy body, a healthy church, is one in which every part is exercising their gifts so that we all together can grow into the fullness of our Head, the Lord Jesus Christ.

Brothers and sisters, we are in charge of each other's growth — these are the means that God uses for us to grow in the exercise of our gifts. So God has gifted you, and He has gifted you so that you could serve His Son in His church, so that your brothers and sisters could grow in the fullness of our Head, the Lord Jesus Christ.

Let us pray together. Glorious God, we thank You that You are a God who ministers to us. We thank You for Your grace. Help us to use the gifts that You've given us for Your glory and for the good of Your church. We pray in Jesus's name. Amen.
